description:
You are acting as a DevOps-focused engineer who has just been given a small
  collection of Kubernetes manifests that a custom operator would normally
  reconcile.  Your goal is to generate a *deterministic* micro-benchmark that
  re-applies (simulated) these manifests in a tight loop, records the duration
  of each iteration, and then produces a summary report.  The final artefacts
  must be saved in a specific directory so that an automated test harness can
  validate them.

  Environment layout that already exists when the session begins
  ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  1. Manifests directory
     /home/user/k8s_manifests
        ├── 01-namespace.yaml
        ├── 02-configmap.yaml
        ├── 03-secret.yaml
        ├── 04-deployment.yaml
        ├── 05-service.yaml
        └── 06-crd.yaml

  2. A simple helper script (simulating the operator’s reconciliation action)
     /home/user/k8s_operator/apply.sh

     The script merely “applies” every *.yaml file passed on its command line
     by concatenating it to /dev/null, so it runs quickly and does not require
     a Kubernetes cluster.

  3. You have normal user privileges; **do not** attempt any `sudo` commands.

  What you must do
  ────────────────
  A. Create a new directory named:
       /home/user/benchmark_results
     (Make sure it exists before writing any files into it.)

  B. Inside that directory run a loop that executes the helper script
     exactly 200 times.  Each loop iteration must:
        1. Invoke the helper script like this:
             /home/user/k8s_operator/apply.sh /home/user/k8s_manifests/*.yaml
        2. Measure only the *elapsed* (wall-clock) run time for the helper
           script.  The precision must be milliseconds (three digits after the
           decimal point).  Typical tools are the external
           `/usr/bin/time -f "%e"` command or `date +%s%3N` snapshots—use what
           you prefer as long as you achieve millisecond precision.
        3. Append one line to the log file described in section “Required log
           file” below.

  C. After finishing the 200-iteration loop, parse the log file you just
     generated and calculate:
        • min_seconds  – the shortest duration observed  
        • max_seconds  – the longest  duration observed  
        • avg_seconds  – arithmetic mean of all 200 durations  
     All three numbers must be written with exactly three digits after the
     decimal point (e.g., 0.006).

  D. Write the calculated statistics to a summary file described in
     “Required summary file” below.

  Required log file
  ─────────────────
  Path: /home/user/benchmark_results/apply_benchmark.log

  • The file must contain exactly 200 lines, one line per loop iteration.
  • Every line must match this *exact* format (including spacing and the order
    of the keys):

        run=<N> duration=<T>

    where:
        <N> is the 1-based iteration counter (an integer from 1 to 200, with
            no leading zeros)
        <T> is the measured elapsed time in seconds rounded to the nearest
            millisecond and printed with exactly three digits after the decimal
            point (e.g., 0.123, 1.007).

  Example of the first three lines (values shown are illustrative):
        run=1 duration=0.005
        run=2 duration=0.004
        run=3 duration=0.006
        …
        run=200 duration=0.004

  Required summary file
  ─────────────────────
  Path: /home/user/benchmark_results/apply_benchmark_summary.txt

  The file must contain **exactly four** lines, in the order shown below:

        total_runs: 200
        min_seconds: <MIN>
        max_seconds: <MAX>
        avg_seconds: <AVG>

  • <MIN>, <MAX> and <AVG> are the values you calculated in step C,
    each printed with exactly three digits after the decimal point.
  • No extra blank lines or whitespace are allowed.

  Verification expectations
  ─────────────────────────
  The automated grader will:
    1. Check that /home/user/benchmark_results exists and is a directory.
    2. Confirm that apply_benchmark.log exists, has 200 lines, and that every
       line follows the strict regex:
          ^run=(?:[1-9]|[1-9][0-9]|1[0-9]{2}|200) duration=[0-9]+\.[0-9]{3}$
       It will also ensure that run numbers start at 1, end at 200, and appear
       exactly once each, in ascending order.
    3. Parse the durations from the 200 lines and validate that the summary
       file contains the correct min, max and average values—rounded/truncated
       exactly as described.
    4. Ensure that apply_benchmark_summary.txt contains precisely the four
       lines shown above, without trailing spaces or blank lines.

  Important notes
  ───────────────
  • Use only POSIX-compatible command-line utilities already present on a
    minimal Debian/Ubuntu installation (bash, awk, sed, cut, sort, bc, date,
    /usr/bin/time, etc.).  Avoid any language runtimes (Python, Go, Node) that
    might not be installed.
  • The benchmark loop must run exactly 200 times—not 199, not 201.
  • Do not modify the manifests themselves; treat them as read-only input.
  • Make sure the benchmark_results directory and both output files are
    writable by the current user (normal permissions are fine).
truth:
<!--
     Ground-truth material used by the automated grader.
     The agent must never see any of this.
  -->
  <files_created_before>
    /home/user/k8s_manifests/01-namespace.yaml:
      ---
      apiVersion: v1
      kind: Namespace
      metadata:
        name: test-namespace

    /home/user/k8s_manifests/02-configmap.yaml:
      ---
      apiVersion: v1
      kind: ConfigMap
      metadata:
        name: test-config
        namespace: test-namespace
      data:
        key: value

    /home/user/k8s_manifests/03-secret.yaml:
      ---
      apiVersion: v1
      kind: Secret
      metadata:
        name: test-secret
        namespace: test-namespace
      stringData:
        password: s3cr3t

    /home/user/k8s_manifests/04-deployment.yaml:
      ---
      apiVersion: apps/v1
      kind: Deployment
      metadata:
        name: test-deploy
        namespace: test-namespace
      spec:
        replicas: 1
        selector:
          matchLabels:
            app: test
        template:
          metadata:
            labels:
              app: test
          spec:
            containers:
              - name: nginx
                image: nginx:1.25-alpine

    /home/user/k8s_manifests/05-service.yaml:
      ---
      apiVersion: v1
      kind: Service
      metadata:
        name: test-svc
        namespace: test-namespace
      spec:
        type: ClusterIP
        selector:
          app: test
        ports:
          - port: 80
            targetPort: 80

    /home/user/k8s_manifests/06-crd.yaml:
      ---
      apiVersion: apiextensions.k8s.io/v1
      kind: CustomResourceDefinition
      metadata:
        name: widgets.example.com
      spec:
        group: example.com
        names:
          kind: Widget
          plural: widgets
          singular: widget
        scope: Namespaced
        versions:
          - name: v1
            served: true
            storage: true
            schema:
              openAPIV3Schema:
                type: object
                properties:
                  spec:
                    type: object
        status:
          acceptedNames: {}
          conditions: []
          storedVersions: []

    /home/user/k8s_operator/apply.sh:
      #!/usr/bin/env bash
      # Simulate "kubectl apply -f ..." by catting the input manifests
      # to /dev/null.  Exit status always 0.
      cat "$@" > /dev/null
  </files_created_before>

  <permissions>
    /home/user/k8s_operator/apply.sh: executable by user
    Everything else: 0644
  </permissions>

  <verification_rules>
    • Directory /home/user/benchmark_results must exist.
    • File /home/user/benchmark_results/apply_benchmark.log:
        – exactly 200 lines
        – each line matches regex:
            ^run=(?:[1-9]|[1-9][0-9]|1[0-9]{2}|200) duration=[0-9]+\.[0-9]{3}$
        – run numbers range 1-200 with no gaps or duplicates.
    • File /home/user/benchmark_results/apply_benchmark_summary.txt
        – exactly four lines, in the fixed order
        – first line literally: total_runs: 200
        – lines 2-4 match regex:
            ^(min_seconds|max_seconds|avg_seconds): [0-9]+\.[0-9]{3}$
        – numeric values correspond to the min/ max/ average computed from
          the 200 durations in the log file (millisecond precision, rounded
          exactly as specified).
  </verification_rules>
